segments.text: The achievement award program we established in 2003 and it's really trying to provide examples of innovative approaches that can be replicated, scaled up by other counties and that's really what we are trying to do is promote just kind of that creative thinking by a lot of counties and their staff and their departments. As the chair said, I'm proud to represent three achievement awards today. this is the 24th annual achievement awards program. We received 103 entries in 2026 and of that we had 40 winners selected from 26 counties, so it's truly a competitive program with less than 40% of all the entries are selected as winners. So a little bit about loudoun county and the history, loudoun county has earned 37 achievement awards and those 37 achievement awards have you ranked tied for third all-time. Now I know MADAM Chair asked me last year where does fairfax rank. >> I was going to ask you this time. Come on, joe. You know I'm going to ask you that question. >> I saw that, I was like she asked me that. >> so not only that, ranked third all-time or tied for third, this is the 14th year in a row since 2013. Really just keeping up in the case. A little bit of the criteria that our judges use. Does the program offer innovative solutions to a problem, situation or delivery of services? Does the program promote intergovernmental cooperation and interdepartmental cooperation? And in fact, one of the trainers got employment within the field, which that I think is just providing really a great link to the community. So that's I think something that I think could be replicated in many of our communities. I know personally we had a dog that we had that was trained in one of our correction facilities, not to be a service dog but I think this is something taking a step further so perhaps this award with the sheriff's office [Inaudible] >> where are we going to hear? >> would you like to come down? Toby over here [Inaudible] >> who wants to talk about this program? >> I'm sorry, I don't know why it won't open. Could you push that release button? >> hi everyone. I'm susan davidson. I-2 Electrical Substations White Paper (Countywide)
Staff Contact(s): Abdul Jaffari & Daniel Galindo, Planning and Zoning
segments.text: >> that was lovely, fun, and it was a dog so it was great. okay, we're going to do item number I 2, which is the electrical substation white paper with the staff please come down, please. >> hello, how are you? Good.

segments.title: Abdul Jaffari with DPZ gave presentation to the Board
segments.text: We are ready when you are. >> all right. Good evening MADAM Chair and supervisors. My name is abdul jaffari. I'm the manager for the data center [Inaudible] Location. Today I'm joined this evening with daniel galindo, director of department of planning and zoning as well as mike and callow and frederico lopez from [Inaudible] Consulting firm, and the purpose of this agenda item is to present the findings of a white paper that summarizes the functions and land-use considerations associated with different types of electrical substations, and the findings of this white paper also inform future land- use policies and regulations related to the substations part of phase two of the data center project. At this stage staff is not prepared to address questions related to the proposed land-use policies and regulations. Those matters would be brought forward to the board considerations at a future meeting. With that I will now turn the

segments.title: Mike with Kimley Horn gave presentation to the Board
segments.text: presentation over to mike and frederico. >> good evening. >> good evening chair randall, vice chair turner and members of the board. My name is mike do niccolo. I'm an engineering project manager with horn and associates and I'm here today with my colleague federica lopez. She's a substation engineer with 30 years experience. We've been retained by the county to support information as it relates to substations. Loudoun county has approximately 95 existing and proposed substations within the county and there's anticipated [Inaudible] Continue to grow. As such the purpose of this white paper is to provide general information to the county and to support the development of policies and regulations around substations. And then this presentation just briefly subsidizes what's in the white paper. This is an agenda for the meeting. We'll start with a review of substations, we'll explain what it is, what a substation is and the different types of substations that exist within the power grid. We'll get into the air insulated substations and gas insulated substations, discuss the pros and cons of each. We'll also go through locations citing considerations for substations. We'll look at substations that service data centers and what are typical screening methods that are used to mitigate visual impacts. With that I'll turn it over to federico to start on the topic of what is a substation. >> thank you, mike. So the first question, what is a substation? Basically a substation is facilitating the power grid that transforms voltage between different levels. Protects and controls the flow of electricity, the flow of power between sources and the end consumers, the users. Next one. So there's different ways to classify substations. We have three classifications here. We're going to start with the first one and that is related to classification of voltage related to the substation. As you can see, we have three categories from transmission, sub transmission and distribution and each one of those have different ranges so if you see transmission under substation has ultrahigh voltage, I'm going to be talking about those ranges in a few seconds, it means transmission substation, the voltage you have in your substations is medium voltage and normally is classified as a distribution substation. The ranges as you can see there, for example, for ultrahigh voltage, is between 100 kv to 1200 kv, kilovolts. 100 kv and 1200 kv, kv is kilovolts. Extra high voltage, 200 to 500 kv. High voltage, 70 to 200 kv approximately. Sub transmission normally between 40 to 70 kv. Voltage 40 kvm low voltage below one kilovolt kv. These ranges, these numbers can change between different utilities so we have to be sometimes careful about when we talk about this application so different abilities or geographical zones with that. The second classification that we have is the substation based on its function. There's four main functions that you can see in substations. Step up, step down, switching and converter. Step up substations are the ones that are most related to elevating the voltage from generation to transmission. Others example here, others aligned to the same generation, renewable energy when you have a solar substation, solar panels, wind farms, so they require collector substations. You have to step up the voltage for the transmission system. Step down is on the other way when you continue the voltage from high-voltage to low- voltage, to feed the user, the customer. Normally for distribution points in plans and data centers. [Inaudible] Classification is switching station. A switching station is related to utilities when utilities have transmission lines that they have to manage the power they have in the substation. The only thing that they are doing is switching the flow inside the substation. That's typical switching substation, utility switching substation. Converter is a substation that normally converts from ac to dc or dc to ac. So there's these four type of classifications of substations based on functions and the last classification that we have here is based on the insulation so that's an introduction. There is two pictures there on the left side. You have an ais substation that's a typical convention substation, the one that everybody sees and 99% of the system, 95% is constructed, a very high level of substation, the systems are insulated. On the other side you have gas insulated substation. We are going to be talking about that substation but it's behind the building that you have all the elements so you don't see it from outside, you don't see the electrical equipment. Thank you. So we're going to go finally a little bit more about what is ais and what is gis. Ais is the primary insulation in some of those components. Just a quick technicality here, when you have two electrical conductors, those electrical conductors, when you put them close together they're going to start sparking so when you see the marking, that's the minimum which can help us by possible design so you don't want to break that distance. The thing that is happening is the insulator that before was doing and insulator between the conductors, now is breaking so with all the substations that you have air insulated substations in the field, they have minimum distances that you have to comply with so they are very bulky and big, you go smaller electrical clearances and you can have issues. It's not safe to do it that way. the eis substation is a substation where you use gas, pressurized gas, sulfur hexafluoride. You use a medium. When you do that, you can reduce that dramatically so that's another thing, it's being used so next one goes into the pros and cons of [ Inaudible] I didn't mention that one of the problems is that it's a greenhouse gas and is 23,000 times stronger than co2 so there's some regulations and that basically can make the use difficult and again, heavily monitored and controlled due to the fact that it's a green gas. Now coming back to the pros and cons, the pros for eis, lower cost, simple design, straightforward visual inspection. It's easier. There is a substation you can see to replace equipment. There is no requirement of any kind of gas insulation. The pros of the eis system is that aesthetics can hide all the equipment behind a building or under a building and the footprints can be reduced dramatically, as I said, up to 70% on the ais footprint. >> thank you. One of the things we were asked to do is to look at the current definition of utility substation which exists within the county zoning ordinance and develop any revisions to that definition or proposed revisions. The original definition had language, nonelectrical substation components in it so it referenced infrastructure such as natural gas and television so those are things that we suggested to remove from that definition and we also looked at it, we thought the terms converter and collector should be added. Federico talked about collector substations earlier, obviously through renewable generation within loudoun county, collector substations would be the substation that would be particularly electrical substation and converters current projects I understand loudoun has a proposed project through one of those so we felt it necessary to include a converter station as a station that falls under electrical substation. And then the other recommendation is there's a lot of discussion about view shed impacts and different types of substations so if it makes sense, we thought it made sense to include definitions for air insulated substation and gas insulated substation, if there's any changes to the code pertaining to either substation type. And here's just -- we get into things a little bit more in the white paper but here just a list of some of the considerations that go into locating, citing substations so obviously federico touched on it a little bit but horizontal and vertical clearances, as required by the national electric safety code requirements. Obviously in terms of topography, geology, flat areas, good drainage outside of floodplains, obviously good

segments.title: ** Saines arrived at 4:53PM**
segments.text: practice, good access from a public road where it could be -- equipment could be installed or trucks could be driven to install equipment and obviously minimizing environmental features as well as water bodies and minimizing impacts of noise and visual impacts to the community. And its safety and economic related items. Federico touched on it a little bit before. I think one of the interesting things is the economics of the gis gas substations, at least equipment is typically more expensive, however, if there's reduction it can be significant reduction in land at times and depending on the price of the land, the cost can make sense for gis in certain arrangements.
